URL: https://github.com/emacs-dashboard/emacs-dashboard/tree/1.7.0
Code/Highlights:
# Emacs Dashboard An extensible emacs startup screen showing you what’s most important.

# Features 1. Register list
2. Org mode agenda
3. Recent projectile projects list (Depends on `projectile` package) 4. Bookmarks list
5. Recent files
6. Displays an awesome Emacs banner! # Screenshot

# Dependencies

You will need the following packages which are all available on Melpa:  ...    3. page-break-lines - https://github.com/purcell/page-break-lines # Usage

```
M-x package-install RET dashboard
``` ```
(require 'dashboard)
(dashboard-setup-startup-hook)
;; Or if you use use-package
(use-package dashboard :ensure t
  :config
  (dashboard-setup-startup-hook))
``` By default, this will show three lists, recent files and bookmarks and org-agenda items. One more widget is implemented  ...    In addition to the above, configure`initial-buffer-choice` to show Dashboard in frames created with`emacsclient -c` as  ...    ```
;; Set the title
(setq dashboard-banner-logo-title "Welcome to Emacs Dashboard")
;; Set the banner (setq dashboard-startup-banner [VALUE])
;; Value can be
;; 'official which displays the official emacs logo  ...    To customize which widgets are displayed, you can use the following snippet ```
(setq dashboard-items '((recents . 5)
                        (bookmarks. 5) (projects. 5)
                        (agenda. 5)
                        (registers. 5))) ``` This will add the recent files, bookmarks, projects, org-agenda and registers widgets to your dashboard each displaying 5 items. To add your own custom widget is pretty easy, define your widget’s callback function and add it to `dashboard-items` as such: ```
(defun dashboard-insert-custom (list-size)
  (insert "Custom text")) (add-to-list 'dashboard-item-generators  '(custom. dashboard-insert-custom)) (add-to-list 'dashboard-items '(custom) t)
``` To add icons to the widget headings and their items:  ...    ```
(add-to-list 'dashboard-items '(agenda) t)
```  ...    This is accomplished with the following steps:

```
# In emacs:
M-x package-delete dashboard-<version> RET
``` ```
make build
make install
```

# evil-multiedit

[](#evil-multiedit)

This plugin was an answer to the lack of proper multiple cursor support in Emacs+evil. It allows you to select and edit matches interactively, integrating `iedit-mode` into evil-mode with an attempt at sensible defaults.

Since then, [evil-mc](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc) has matured, and now that I use both I've found they can coexist, filling different niches, complimenting evil's built-in column/line-wise editing operations.

[![evil-multiedit](/hlissner/evil-multiedit/raw/screenshots/main.gif?raw=true)](/hlissner/evil-multiedit/blob/screenshots/main.gif?raw=true)

> Thanks to [syl20bnr](https://github.com/syl20bnr) for his [evil-iedit-state](https://github.com/syl20bnr/evil-iedit-state) plugin, which this plugin was heavily inspired by.

## Installation

[](#installation)

The package is available on MELPA.

`M-x package-install RET evil-multiedit`

Then load it up with the default keybinds:

(require 'evil-multiedit)
(evil-multiedit-default-keybinds)

### Doom Emacs

[](#doom-emacs)

This package comes pre-configured with [Doom Emacs](https://github.com/hlissner/doom-emacs), in its `:editor multiple-cursors` module. [Enable this module](https://github.com/hlissner/doom-emacs/blob/develop/docs/getting_started.org#modules) and you're good to go!

## Usage

[](#usage)

Evil-multiedit does not automatically bind any keys. Call `(evil-multiedit-default-keybinds)` to bind my recommended configuration:

;; Highlights all matches of the selection in the buffer.
(define-key evil-visual-state-map "R" 'evil-multiedit-match-all)

;; Match the word under cursor (i.e. make it an edit region). Consecutive presses will
;; incrementally add the next unmatched match.
(define-key evil-normal-state-map (kbd "M-d") 'evil-multiedit-match-and-next)
;; Match selected region.
(define-key evil-visual-state-map (kbd "M-d") 'evil-multiedit-match-and-next)
;; Insert marker at point
(define-key evil-insert-state-map (kbd "M-d") 'evil-multiedit-toggle-marker-here)

;; Same as M-d but in reverse.
(define-key evil-normal-state-map (kbd "M-D") 'evil-multiedit-match-and-prev)
(define-key evil-visual-state-map (kbd "M-D") 'evil-multiedit-match-and-prev)

;; OPTIONAL: If you prefer to grab symbols rather than words, use
;; \`evil-multiedit-match-symbol-and-next\` (or prev).

;; Restore the last group of multiedit regions.
(define-key evil-visual-state-map (kbd "C-M-D") 'evil-multiedit-restore)

;; RET will toggle the region under the cursor
(define-key evil-multiedit-state-map (kbd "RET") 'evil-multiedit-toggle-or-restrict-region)

;; ...and in visual mode, RET will disable all fields outside the selected region
(define-key evil-motion-state-map (kbd "RET") 'evil-multiedit-toggle-or-restrict-region)

;; For moving between edit regions
(define-key evil-multiedit-state-map (kbd "C-n") 'evil-multiedit-next)
(define-key evil-multiedit-state-map (kbd "C-p") 'evil-multiedit-prev)
(define-key evil-multiedit-insert-state-map (kbd "C-n") 'evil-multiedit-next)
(define-key evil-multiedit-insert-state-map (kbd "C-p") 'evil-multiedit-prev)

;; Ex command that allows you to invoke evil-multiedit with a regular expression, e.g.
(evil-ex-define-cmd "ie\[dit\]" 'evil-multiedit-ex-match)

Once regions are highlighted, changes are mirrored across them all.

Many evil-mode motions/operators will have slightly different behavior while evil-multiedit is active or the cursor is in an iedit region:

-   `D`: clear the region
-   `C`: clear to end-of-region and go into insert mode
-   `A`: go into insert mode at end-of-region
-   `I`: go into insert mode at start-of-region
-   `V`: select the region
-   `P`: replace the iedit region with the contents of the clipboard
-   `$`: go to end-of-region
-   `0`/`^`: go to start-of-region
-   `gg`/`G`: go to the first/last region

To disable these, set `evil-multiedit-dwim-motion-keys` to `nil` before loading evil-multiedit.

NOTE: No need to bind a key for `evil-multiedit-abort`, pressing ESC in normal mode will invoke it.

### Ex Command

[](#ex-command)

`:iedit [REGEXP]` is available for invoking multiedit from the ex command line.

### Commands

[](#commands)

-   `evil-multiedit-restore`: Restore the last evil-multiedit session.
-   `evil-multiedit-match-all`: Create iedit regions of all matches of the current selection (or symbol at point) as multiedit regions.
-   `evil-multiedit-match-and-next`:
-   `evil-multiedit-match-and-prev`
-   `evil-multiedit-match-symbol-and-next`
-   `evil-multiedit-match-symbol-and-prev`
-   `evil-multiedit-toggle-marker-here`
-   `evil-multiedit-toggle-or-restrict-region`
-   `evil-multiedit-next`
-   `evil-multiedit-prev`
-   `evil-multiedit-abort`
-   `evil-multiedit-ex-match`

### Options

[](#options)

-   `evil-multiedit-dwim-motion-keys` (default: `t`): If non-nil, evil's motion keys behave differently when the point is inside a multiedit region. Must be set before evil-multiedit is loaded.
-   `evil-multiedit-ignore-indent-and-trailing` (default: `t`): If non-nil, skip over indentation and trailing whitespace in iedit matches.
-   `evil-multiedit-scope` (default `nil`): How far evil-multiedit should look for incremental matches (doesn't affect `evil-multiedit-match-all` or `evil-multiedit-ex-match`). Accepts anything that `bounds-of-thing-at-point` accepts, such as `'defun`, `'sexp`, `'email` or the default, `'buffer`.
-   `evil-multiedit-smart-match-boundaries` (default `t`): If non-nil, multiedit will treat matches as atoms when invoked from normal mode. E.g.
    -   `evil-multiedit-match` will not match `evil-multiedit-match-all`
    -   `i` will only match `i` and not every individual i in `ignition`.
    -   **NOTE:** If evil-multiedit is invoked from visual mode, this is ignored.
-   `evil-multiedit-store-in-search-history` (default `nil`): If non-nil, highlighted occurrences are stored in `regexp-search-ring`, so that after exiting iedit `evil-search-next` and `evil-search-previous` (usually n and N) use the last occurrence as if it were the last string in the search history.
-   `evil-multiedit-follow-matches` (default `nil`): If non-nil, the cursor will jump to each additional match, rather than remain in its original position.

### Co-existing with evil-mc

[](#co-existing-with-evil-mc)

How the two plugins mingle is entirely personal preference. I often reach for evil-mc for more complex operations and evil-multiedit for simpler ones.

My strategy is to bind evil-multiedit to M-d/M-D, and evil-mc to a bunch of keys prefixed with gz:

;; evil-multiedit
(evil-define-key 'normal 'global
  (kbd "M-d")   #'evil-multiedit-match-symbol-and-next
  (kbd "M-D")   #'evil-multiedit-match-symbol-and-prev)
(evil-define-key 'visual 'global
  "R"           #'evil-multiedit-match-all
  (kbd "M-d")   #'evil-multiedit-match-and-next
  (kbd "M-D")   #'evil-multiedit-match-and-prev)
(evil-define-key '(visual normal) 'global
  (kbd "C-M-d") #'evil-multiedit-restore)

(with-eval-after-load 'evil-mutliedit
  (evil-define-key 'multiedit 'global
    (kbd "M-d")   #'evil-multiedit-match-and-next
    (kbd "M-S-d") #'evil-multiedit-match-and-prev
    (kbd "RET")   #'evil-multiedit-toggle-or-restrict-region)
  (evil-define-key '(multiedit multiedit-insert) 'global
    (kbd "C-n")   #'evil-multiedit-next
    (kbd "C-p")   #'evil-multiedit-prev))

;; evil-mc
(evil-define-key '(normal visual) 'global
  "gzm" #'evil-mc-make-all-cursors
  "gzu" #'evil-mc-undo-all-cursors
  "gzz" #'+evil/mc-toggle-cursors
  "gzc" #'+evil/mc-make-cursor-here
  "gzn" #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-next-cursor
  "gzp" #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-prev-cursor
  "gzN" #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-last-cursor
  "gzP" #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-first-cursor)
(with-eval-after-load 'evil-mc
  (evil-define-key '(normal visual) evil-mc-key-map
    (kbd "C-n") #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-next-cursor
    (kbd "C-N") #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-last-cursor
    (kbd "C-p") #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-prev-cursor
    (kbd "C-P") #'evil-mc-make-and-goto-first-cursor))

NOTE: These are the default keybindings on [Doom Emacs](https://github.com/hlissner/doom-emacs). Doom users don't need to bind these.

## Multiple cursors implementation for evil-mode

[](#multiple-cursors-implementation-for-evil-mode)

### Synopsis

[](#synopsis)

**evil-mc** provides multiple cursors functionality for Emacs when used with **evil-mode**

### Usage

[](#usage)

Start with:

(require 'evil-mc)

#### Local Setup

[](#local-setup)

To enable or disable **evil-mc** mode for a single buffer use:  

(evil-mc-mode  1) ;; enable
(evil-mc-mode \-1) ;; disable

#### Global Setup

[](#global-setup)

To enable or disable **evil-mc** mode for all buffers use:  

(global-evil-mc-mode  1) ;; enable
(global-evil-mc-mode \-1) ;; disable

#### Basic Usage

[](#basic-usage)

The main commands used to create or delete cursors are:

(evil-mc-make-all-cursors)
;; Create cursors for all strings that match the selected 
;; region or the symbol under cursor.

(evil-mc-undo-all-cursors)
;; Remove all cursors.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-next-match)
;; Make a cursor at point and go to the next match of the 
;; selected region or the symbol under cursor.

(evil-mc-skip-and-goto-next-match)
;; Go to the next match of the selected region or the symbol under 
;; cursor without creating a cursor at point.

The above commands as well as others, detailed below, are setup with key bindings when the **evil-mc** mode is enabled. The keys are defined in `evil-mc-key-map`. You can take a look at that variable declaration in [evil-mc.el](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/evil-mc.el) to see all key bindings. But, in short, `C-n` / `C-p` are used for creating cursors, and `M-n` / `M-p` are used for cycling through cursors. The commands that create cursors wrap around; but, the ones that cycle them do not. To skip creating a cursor forward use `C-t` or `grn` and backward `grp`. Finally use `gru` to “undo” the last added cursor, and `grq` to remove all cursors.

Optionally set up visual mode keybindings for `I` and `A` to create cursors at the beginning or end of every visually selected line.

(evil-define-key 'visual evil-mc-key-map
  "A" #'evil-mc-make-cursor-in-visual-selection-end
  "I" #'evil-mc-make-cursor-in-visual-selection-beg)

For an example of setting up **evil-mc** see this [setup file](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/evil-mc-setup.el)

#### Commands

[](#commands)

Here’s a detailed list of all commands used to create, navigate through, or delete cursors:  
*All the commands below assume that there is a real cursor and possibly some fake cursors.*

(evil-mc-make-all-cursors)
;; Make a cursor for every match of the selected region or the symbol at point.

(evil-mc-undo-all-cursors)
;; Remove all cursors.

(evil-mc-undo-last-added-cursor)
;; Remove the last added cursor and move point to its position.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-next-match)
;; Make a cursor at point, and go to the next match of the 
;; selected region or the symbol at point.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-prev-match)
;; Make a cursor at point, and go to the previous match of the 
;; selected region or the symbol at point.

(evil-mc-skip-and-goto-next-match)
;; Go to the next match of the selected region or symbol at point
;; without making a cursor at point. This command can be used to
;; remove unwanted cursors.

(evil-mc-skip-and-goto-prev-match)
;; Go to the previous match of the selected region or symbol at point
;; without making a cursor at point. This command can be used to
;; remove unwanted cursors.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-prev-cursor)
;; Make a cursor at point and move point to the cursor
;; closest to it when searching backwards.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-next-cursor)
;; Make a cursor at point and move point to the cursor
;; closest to it when searching forwards.

(evil-mc-skip-and-goto-prev-cursor)
;; Move point to the cursor closest to it when searching backwards
;; without making a cursor at point. This command can be used to
;; remove unwanted cursors.

(evil-mc-skip-and-goto-next-cursor)
;; Move point to the cursor closest to it when searching forwards
;; without making a cursor at point. This command can be used to
;; remove unwanted cursors.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-first-cursor)
;; Make a cursor at point and move point to the cursor at the first position.

(evil-mc-make-and-goto-last-cursor)
;; Make a cursor at point and move point to the cursor at the last position.

(evil-mc-make-cursor-here)
;; Create a cursor at point. This command should be used with \`evil-mc-pause-cursors'.

(evil-mc-pause-cursors)
;; Pause all fake cursors. This can be used with \`evil-mc-make-cursor-here'

(evil-mc-resume-cursors)
;; Call to resume paused cursors.

(evil-mc-make-cursor-in-visual-selection-beg)
;; Create cursors at the beginning of every visually selected line.

(evil-mc-make-cursor-in-visual-selection-end)
;; Create cursors at the end of every visually selected line.

#### Customization

[](#customization)

**evil-mc** can be customized in several ways:

-   Every known command is executed using a command handler defined in a variable called `evil-mc-known-commands` in [evil-mc-known-commands.el](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/evil-mc-known-commands.el). Those can be overridden by defining the `evil-mc-custom-known-commands` variable. See the documentation of that variable in [evil-mc.el](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/evil-mc.el) for more info.
-   Some minor modes are incompatible with **evil-mc**. Those modes are defined in `evil-mc-incompatible-minor-modes` and can be overridden by defining that variable.
-   In addition there are two hooks that can be used to temporarily disable or enable additional functionality while there are multiple cursors active
    
    evil-mc-before-cursors-created
    ;; this hook runs just before the first cursor is created
    
    evil-mc-after-cursors-created
    ;; this hook runs just after the last cursor is deleted
        
    

### Mode line text and colors

[](#mode-line-text-and-colors)

There are 4 variables, that can change the mode lines text, and its color.

#### Only one cursor

[](#only-one-cursor)

The `emc` text can be hidden, by setting this variable to `nil`. (default: `t`)

(setq evil-mc-one-cursor-show-mode-line-text t)

#### Two or more cursors, resumed (unpaused)

[](#two-or-more-cursors-resumed-unpaused)

The resumed mode line text, can have two different colors:

-   The cursors color, when this variable is `t`.
-   The default colors, when this variable is `nil`.

(default: `t`)

(setq evil-mc-mode-line-text-cursor-color t)

#### Two or more cursors, paused

[](#two-or-more-cursors-paused)

The `(paused)` text can be hidden, by setting this variable to `nil`. (default: `t`)

(setq evil-mc-mode-line-text-paused t)

The paused mode line text can have three different colors:

-   Inverse colors, when the inverse colors variable is `t`.
-   Cursors color, when the inverse colors variable is `nil`, and the cursor color variable is `t`.
-   Default colors, when both the inverse and cursor color variables are `nil`.

(default: `t`, for both the inverse and cursor variables)

(setq evil-mc-mode-line-text-inverse-colors t)
(setq evil-mc-mode-line-text-cursor-color t)

### Notes

[](#notes)

-   Most evil motions and operators are supported but not every single command will work.
-   If the cursors don’t seem to work during a command, either the command is not known (see `evil-mc-known-commands` in [evil-mc-known-commands.el](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/evil-mc-known-commands.el)) or some minor modes could be interfering with the evil-mc operations.
-   Issues and pull requests are welcome.

#### Debugging

[](#debugging)

-   When a command does not work, and you want to get more information, you can enable (or disable) debugging by running any of the commands below interactively.
    
    (evil-mc-executing-debug-on)
    ;; Turn debug on while executing a command.
    
    (evil-mc-executing-debug-off)
    ;; Turn debug off while executing a command.
    
    (evil-mc-recording-debug-on)
    ;; Turn debug on while recording a command.
    
    (evil-mc-recording-debug-off)
    ;; Turn debug off while recording a command.
    
    (evil-mc-all-debug-on)
    ;; Turn all debug on.
    
    (evil-mc-all-debug-off)
    ;; Turn all debug off.
        
    

#### Limitations

[](#limitations)

-   After an undo command the cursors will return to their original positions if [undo-tree](http://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/UndoTree) mode is enabled and `evil-repeat` has not been used.
-   Redo may cause the real cursor to get out of sync with the others. This can be worked around by setting a mark and returning to it after a redo.
-   Jumps work if [evil-jumper](https://github.com/bling/evil-jumper) mode is enabled
-   Search commands such as `evil-search-forward`, `evil-search-backward`, and `evil-search-next` are not supported

#### Known issues

[](#known-issues)

-   Only named commands can be executed by the fake cursors.
-   There could be a performance penalty when there are too many cursors (30+).
-   Paste will not work when [spacemacs](https://github.com/syl20bnr/spacemacs)’ paste micro state is enabled. This is due to the fact that `evil-paste-pop` and `evil-paste-pop-next` commands are not supported.
-   `evil-repeat` works only for some commands. In particular it doesn’t work for delete. It will also interfere with the cursor positions during an undo or redo operation.

## Short version

- **evil-mc** is the more literal **multiple-cursors** package for Evil:
  you create many cursors and mirror commands across them.
  Better for **heterogeneous / more complex edits**.
  Docs also admit more edge cases and compatibility caveats.
  Sources:
  [README](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/README.org),
  [repo page](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc)

- **evil-multiedit** is closer to **interactive region-based substitution** via `iedit` + Evil:
  you mark matching regions, then edits propagate across those regions.
  Better for **quick, homogeneous edits on repeated text**.
  Its own README explicitly says it can **coexist with evil-mc** and fill a different niche.
  Sources:
  [README](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/blob/master/README.md),
  [comparison note](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/issues/14),
  [repo page](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit)

## Package-by-package

| Package | Purpose | Main workflow | Key commands/concepts | Better fit when |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **evil-mc** | True Evil multiple cursors | Create/skip/remove cursors, then execute commands across them | `evil-mc-mode`, `global-evil-mc-mode`, `evil-mc-make-all-cursors`, `evil-mc-make-and-goto-next-match`, `evil-mc-skip-and-goto-next-match`, pause/resume cursors; default map uses `C-n`/`C-p`, `M-n`/`M-p`, `gr...` | You need cursor-by-cursor positioning, more complex edits, or line/column-style multi-cursor work |
| **evil-multiedit** | Evil front-end for `iedit`-style match editing | Select a word/region, create highlighted edit regions, then edits mirror across them | `evil-multiedit-default-keybinds`, `M-d`/`M-D`, `R` for match-all, `RET` to toggle/restrict region, `C-n`/`C-p` to move, `:iedit [REGEXP]` | You want quick repeated-text edits with less cursor bookkeeping |

## Main documented difference

### evil-mc
- Thinks in **cursors**.
- The README emphasizes command execution across fake cursors and exposes cursor lifecycle operations:
  create, skip, undo last, undo all, pause, resume, place cursors at visual selection boundaries.
- It also documents command-handler customization and incompatible minor modes.
  Source:
  [evil-mc README](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/README.org)

### evil-multiedit
- Thinks in **matched edit regions**.
- The README says it integrates **`iedit-mode` into evil-mode**.
- Once regions are active, many Evil motions/operators get region-aware behavior.
- It offers a very discoverable workflow around “match next/prev”, “match all”, “restrict region”, and `:iedit`.
  Source:
  [evil-multiedit README](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/blob/master/README.md)

## Overlap / coexistence

- They **overlap** in solving “edit several places at once in Evil”.
- `evil-multiedit`’s README explicitly says **evil-mc has matured** and that the author uses **both**, because they “fill different niches”.
- It also includes a section on **co-existing with evil-mc** with a suggested split of keybindings.
  Source:
  [evil-multiedit README, co-existing with evil-mc](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/blob/master/README.md#co-existing-with-evil-mc)

## Which one when?

- Pick **evil-multiedit** if your common task is:
  “I want to quickly edit all/next matching occurrences of this thing.”
- Pick **evil-mc** if your common task is:
  “I need several real cursors because the edits/motions at each position are more involved.”

A nice one-line summary from `evil-multiedit`’s author:
- **evil-multiedit** is better for **quick edits on homogeneous text**
- **evil-mc** is better for **complex edits on heterogeneous text**
  Source:
  [issue #14](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/issues/14)

## Maintenance / status signals visible from the repos

- **evil-mc** looks **more active**:
  public repo metadata shows a more recent last push, more contributors, and the README has a larger “limitations / known issues / customization” surface.
  Source:
  [repo page](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc)

- **evil-multiedit** looks **more stable / quieter**, but also less active:
  repo metadata shows an older last push.
  Its README already frames it as complementary to `evil-mc`, not as the one package to replace it.
  Source:
  [repo page](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit),
  [README](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/blob/master/README.md)

- Minor signal for both:
  both READMEs still show **Travis CI** badges, which is a bit of an “older project” smell.
  Sources:
  [evil-mc README](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/master/README.org),
  [evil-multiedit README](https://github.com/hlissner/evil-multiedit/blob/master/README.md)

## Recommendation for your Emacs workflow

- If you want the simplest repeated-edit workflow in Evil, start with **evil-multiedit**.
- If you want the more capable long-term multi-cursor tool, use **evil-mc**.
- If your setup can tolerate both, the docs explicitly support the idea of **using both for different jobs**.

Best beginner path for **evil-mc**:

1. **Turn it on**
   Practice enabling it per buffer first, then globally once it feels natural.  
   Teaches: evil-mc is a minor mode, not a one-shot command.  
   Source: [README.org#L18-L33](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L18-L33)

2. **`C-n` / `C-p` — add next/previous matching cursor**
   Start on a symbol, or visually select text first, then grow to the next/previous match.  
   Teaches: the core evil-mc workflow is *progressive cursor creation from a symbol/selection*.  
   Source: [README.org#L34-L61](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L34-L61), [evil-mc.el#L99-L107](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/evil-mc.el#L99-L107)

3. **`C-t` / `grn` — skip a bad match**
   When the next occurrence shouldn’t be edited, skip it instead of aborting.  
   Teaches: precise selection control; this is the key habit that makes evil-mc feel usable.  
   Source: [README.org#L57-L61](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L57-L61), [README.org#L95-L103](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L95-L103)

4. **`gru` / `grq` — undo last cursor, then clear all**
   Practice backing out one cursor, then all cursors.  
   Teaches: recovery and clean exit.  
   Source: [README.org#L60-L61](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L60-L61), [README.org#L81-L85](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L81-L85)

5. **Visual `I` / `A` — one cursor per selected line**
   Use a visual line/block selection, then place cursors at line starts or ends.  
   Teaches: linewise multi-editing, good for adding prefixes/suffixes.  
   Source: [README.org#L63-L70](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L63-L70), [README.org#L138-L142](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L138-L142)

### What each step teaches
- **1**: setup/model
- **2**: grow matches
- **3**: prune matches
- **4**: recover/exit
- **5**: linewise editing

### Common beginner pitfalls / limitations
- **Creation wraps; cursor cycling doesn’t.**  
  `C-n`/`C-p` wrap around, but `M-n`/`M-p` only cycle existing cursors without wrapping.  
  Source: [README.org#L54-L61](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L54-L61)

- **Not every Evil command works.**  
  The README explicitly says most motions/operators are supported, but not all; unknown commands or minor modes can interfere.  
  Source: [README.org#L200-L205](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L200-L205)

- **Search commands are not supported.**  
  Don’t train with `evil-search-forward/backward/next` as part of the multi-cursor workflow.  
  Source: [README.org#L231-L237](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L231-L237)

- **Undo/redo and `evil-repeat` are shaky.**  
  Undo can reset cursor positions, redo can desync the real cursor, and `evil-repeat` only works for some commands.  
  Source: [README.org#L231-L247](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L231-L247)

- **Too many cursors hurts.**  
  The docs call out a performance penalty around **30+ cursors**.  
  Source: [README.org#L239-L241](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/README.org#L239-L241)

- **Practical setup often disables interfering behavior.**  
  The sample setup temporarily turns off things like smartchr/whitespace cleanup while cursors are active.  
  Good hint for Doom: if something behaves weirdly, suspect a minor mode first.  
  Source: [evil-mc-setup.el#L25-L46](https://github.com/gabesoft/evil-mc/blob/7e363dd6b0a39751e13eb76f2e9b7b13c7054a43/evil-mc-setup.el#L25-L46)

Tiny contrast with evil-multiedit:
- evil-multiedit feels more like a “match session”.
- evil-mc rewards learning **add / skip / undo / linewise cursors** explicitly.

A good way to practice it in Doom is to use three tiny drills.

### Drill 1: repeated symbol rename
Put point on a word like `status`.
Press `C-n` a few times.
Use `C-t` to skip one occurrence.
Then change them together with a simple insertion or replace.
This builds the core habit: **grow, skip, edit, clear**.

### Drill 2: add a prefix to many lines
Select 4–8 lines in visual-line mode.
Press `I` to put cursors at line starts.
Insert something like `// ` or `- `.
Then exit back to normal mode and clear cursors if needed.
This is usually the fastest “aha” moment for evil-mc.

### Drill 3: add a suffix to many lines
Again select several lines.
Press `A` to place cursors at line ends.
Append `,` or `;`.
This teaches that evil-mc is very strong for structured line edits, not just match-based edits.

### Suggested beginner command set in Doom
If Doom already gives you the default bindings, I’d learn these first and ignore the rest for now:
- `C-n` — add next match
- `C-p` — add previous match
- `C-t` — skip next match
- `gru` — remove last cursor
- `grq` — remove all cursors
- `I` / `A` in visual selection — one cursor per selected line

### Practical advice
Use evil-mc for:
- repeated symbol/text edits in one buffer
- adding/removing prefixes or suffixes
- small structured edits where you can still visually verify all cursors

Do **not** start with:
- huge match sets
- undo/redo-heavy workflows
- search-driven workflows
- commands that rely on plugin-specific interactive behavior

### Mental model
The clean beginner model is:
- **evil-multiedit** = “select a set of matches”
- **evil-mc** = “manually build a cursor set and edit through Evil”

That difference is why evil-mc feels more mechanical at first, but also more flexible once the cursor-building commands become muscle memory.

If you want, I can continue with a **10-minute practice routine** or a **Doom config snippet that exposes the most useful evil-mc commands clearly**.

Here is a compact **10-minute practice routine**.

### Minute 1–2: single-word expansion
In a scratch buffer, write the same word 6–8 times.
Put point on one occurrence.
Press `C-n` repeatedly.
Then `grq` to clear all cursors.
Goal: get comfortable with how matches are added.

### Minute 3–4: skip bad matches
Use a buffer where one or two occurrences should stay unchanged.
Press `C-n`, then `C-t` when you hit one you want to ignore.
Edit the remaining ones.
Goal: learn that **skip** is part of normal use, not an edge case.

### Minute 5–6: line starts
Create 5 short lines.
Select them in visual-line mode.
Press `I`, insert a prefix, exit insert.
Goal: linewise edits should start feeling automatic.

### Minute 7–8: line ends
On the same lines, visually select again.
Press `A`, append a suffix.
Goal: practice the second half of linewise editing.

### Minute 9: recover
Add too many cursors on purpose.
Use `gru` a few times.
Then `grq`.
Goal: learn recovery so mistakes feel cheap.

### Minute 10: realistic edit
Take a small config snippet or code block.
Use:
- `C-n` to grow matches
- `C-t` to skip
- `I` or `A` for line edits
Goal: combine the three patterns in one pass.

### Beginner rules of thumb
- Prefer **5–10 cursors**, not 30+.
- Prefer **simple insert/change/delete** operations first.
- If behavior feels odd, suspect unsupported commands or minor-mode interference before blaming yourself.
- Don’t build your early workflow around undo/redo or search commands.

### Doom-friendly learning order
1. `C-n`
2. `C-t`
3. `gru`
4. `grq`
5. visual `I`
6. visual `A`

When running OpenCode inside terminal multiplexers like Zellij or tmux, the TUI's mouse capture prevents normal copy operations: 1. **Mouse selection** - captured by TUI, can't use native terminal selection 2. **Ctrl+C** - exits OpenCode instead of copying selected text While `Shift+mouse` works as a workaround in some terminals, this is not intuitive and doesn't work consistently across  ...    ## Proposal

Add a configuration option to disable mouse capture entirely: ```json
// opencode.json
{
  "tui": {
    "mouse": false
  }
}
```

Or via environment variable: ```bash
OPENCODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=true opencode
```

## Rationale OpenCode TUI doesn't heavily rely on mouse interactions - the primary workflow is keyboard-driven. Users who prefer mouse-based text selection over TUI mouse features should have the option to disable capture. ## Environment

- Terminal: Zellij / tmux
- OS: Linux
- OpenCode version: latest

### Description The TUI currently does not support mouse scroll for navigating through messages. Users have to rely on keyboard shortcuts (e.g. `PageUp`/`PageDown`, `Ctrl+Alt+Y`/`Ctrl+Alt+E`) to scroll, which is less intuitive compared to using the mouse wheel.  ...    Users should be able to scroll through the conversation history using the mouse wheel, similar to how most modern terminal applications (e.g. `htop`, `lazygit`) handle mouse input. ### Current Workaround

Use keyboard shortcuts to scroll: - `PageUp` / `PageDown` — scroll by page
- `Ctrl+Alt+Y` / `Ctrl+Alt+E` — scroll line by line - `Ctrl+Alt+U` / `Ctrl+Alt+D` — scroll half page ### Suggested Implementation Enable mouse mode in the TUI (e.g. via the underlying terminal library) and handle scroll wheel events (`MouseScrollUp` / `MouseScrollDown`) to trigger the existing scroll logic. Optionally, this could be made configurable in `tui.json`:

```json
{
  "mouse_scroll": true
}
```

### Environment - Platform: Linux
